title: The present simple of be: Form
description:
The present simple of the verb be has three forms: ‣ I am.
‣ He/She/It is.
‣ We/You/They are.

rules.title: They= people and things
rules.explanation: She‘s beautiful.

examples.sentence: We use he for a man, she for a woman, and it for a thing.
He‘s a little boy.
I like this TV. It‘s very big.
We use they for people and for things.
I love Sara and Jonas. They are my friends.
I love these chairs. They are very beautiful.

rules.title: Don’t forget the subject
rules.explanation: Is cold.

examples.sentence: We always need a subject before the verb.
It is cold.
Sally is a wonderful woman.
Is a wonderful woman.

rules.title: Contractions or short forms
rules.explanation: We use ‘m, ‘s and ‘re with personal pronouns (I, you, he, etc.) I’m sad. You’re tired. Laura’s beautiful.

examples.sentence: She’s from Scotland.
But we can also use ‘s with names.
London’s an expensive city.

rules.title: Contractions in short answers
rules.explanation: Yes, I’m. Yes, she’s. Yes, they’re.

examples.sentence: We can only use contractions in negative short answers. Not in positive short answers.
Yes, I am.
Yes, she is.
Yes, they are.

title: The present simple of be – USE
description: We use the verb be to talk about: Who we are: I‘m Steven, and this is Isabella. And this is my daughter, Alicia. Age: I‘m 24 years old, and my father is 50. Job: I‘m a teacher, and my wife is a doctor. Nationality: Alex is from Ireland, and Sonia is from Spain. Feelings: I‘m scared. She is very tired. Colour: Our dog is black. His eyes are blue. Prices: It‘s seven pounds. This T-shirt is twenty dollars. The weather: It‘s sunny today. It is very cold this morning. The time: What time is it? It‘s ten past four. Descriptions in general: He is very short and thin. The cars are old and cheap.

title: Present simple form
description: In present simple verbs, we need to use the verb do/does in questions and don’t/doesn’t in negatives.

examples.sentence: The form is the same for all the other persons except he/she/it. The spelling for he/she/it is different.

title: Present simple use
description: We use present simple for habits or actions that we repeat regularly:

rules.title: Habits or actions that happen regularly
rules.explanation: We use present simple for habits or actions that we repeat regularly:

examples.sentence: I wash my hair every day.
I never go to the library.
I go to the library on Saturdays.

rules.title: Permanent situations or things that are always or often true

examples.sentence: I don’t drink coffee.
She’s very tall.
I have two brothers.
Water boils at 100 degrees.
I like soup.

title: Present continuous: Form
description: We form the present continuous with am/is/are + the -ing form of the verb. I am writing a book. She is listening to the radio. They are doing their homework. The short forms of am/is/are are: ‘m/’s/’re. I‘m writing a book. She‘s listening to the radio. They‘re doing their homework. The negative short forms of am not/is not/are not are: ‘m not/isn’t/aren’t. I‘m not writing a book. She isn’t listening to the radio. They aren’t doing their homework. ❗ Note that we can use short forms only in negative short answers, but not in positive short answers. ‘Are you writing a book?’ ‘No, I’m not/I am not.’ ‘Are you writing a book?’ ‘Yes, I am.’ ‘Are you writing a book?’ ‘Yes, I’m.’

rules.title: Actions happening now
rules.explanation: ‘What are you doing?’ ‘I‘m watching TV.’

examples.sentence: We use the present continuous for things happening now, at the moment of speaking.

rules.title: Actions happening around now
rules.explanation: We use the present continuous for temporary things that are happening around now (=not at the moment of speaking but these days, or weeks, etc.) I‘m reading a very good book at the moment. (=I’m not reading it at the moment I’m speaking, but I’m reading it these days.)

rules.title: Now, these days, at the moment, etc.

examples.sentence: We use the present continuous with time expressions that mean ‘now’ or ‘around now’: now, at the moment, at present, today, these days, this week, etc.

title: Present simple or continuous?: Grammar chart
Present simple
description: We use the present simple for habits or permanent situations. You can revise the form of the present simple tense here.

rules.title: Habits or actions that happen regularly
rules.explanation: We use the present simple for habits or actions that we repeat regularly:

examples.sentence: I wash my hair every day.
I never go to the library.
I go to the library on Saturdays.

rules.title: Permanent situations or things that are always or often true

examples.sentence: I don’t drink coffee.
She’s very tall.
I have two brothers.
Water boils at 100 degrees.
I like soup.
We use adverbs and expressions of frequency with the present simple to talk about how often something happens.
We usually order a pizza on Fridays.
I go running twice a week.

rules.title: Stative or non-action verbs
rules.explanation: Some verbs don’t describe actions. These verbs are called stative or non-action verbs, and we can use these verbs in the present simple but NOT in the present continuous. Some of these verbs are be, have (=possess/own), like, love, hate, prefer, need, want, know, etc.

examples.sentence: They have a new car.
They are having a new car.
I like chocolate.
I‘m liking chocolate.

title: Present continuous
description: We use the present continuous for things that are happening now (at the moment of speaking) or for temporary things that are happening around now (these days or weeks, etc.). You can revise the form of the present continuous tense here.

rules.title: Actions happening now
rules.explanation: ‘What are you doing?’ ‘I‘m watching TV.’

examples.sentence: We use the present continuous for things happening now, at the moment of speaking.

rules.title: Actions happening around now
rules.explanation: We use the present continuous for temporary things that are happening around now (=not at the moment of speaking but these days, weeks, etc.) I‘m reading a very good book at the moment. (=I’m not reading it at the moment I’m speaking, but I’m reading it these days.) What TV series are you watching these days? (=You are not watching it at the moment of speaking, but these days.)

rules.title: Now, these days, at the moment, etc.

examples.sentence: We use the present continuous with time expressions that mean now or around now. Some of these expressions are: now, at the moment, at present, today, these days, this week, etc.

title: Present simple or continuous?
description: I listen to the radio. (=I usually listen to the radio; it’s a habit.) I‘m listening to the radio. (=I’m doing it now.)

rules.title: Usually or now?
rules.explanation: I listen to the radio. (=I usually listen to the radio; it’s a habit.) I‘m listening to the radio. (=I’m doing it now.)

examples.sentence: We use the present simple for things that we usually do, and we use the present continuous for things that we are doing now.
I don’t usually watch documentaries, but I’m watching a documentary now.

rules.title: Temporary or permanent?
rules.explanation: I work in an office. (=Permanent situation.) I‘m working in an office. (=Temporary situation.) I live in Edinburgh. (=Permanent situation.) I‘m living in Edinburgh. (=Temporary situation.)

examples.sentence: We use present simple when a situation is permanent or present continuous when a situation is temporary.

rules.title: What do you do? vs What are you doing?
rules.explanation: What does Erik do? (=What is his job?) What is Erik doing? (=What is he doing now, at the moment of speaking?)

examples.sentence: The verb have got is a common form used in the UK, mainly when people are speaking. In the US, people use the verb have.
I‘ve got a car. = I have a car.
I haven’t got a car. = I don’t have a car.
Have you got a car? = Do you have a car?

rules.title: Be careful with this common mistake!

examples.sentence: The verb have got only has a present form (NOT past).
I had got a car.
I had a car.
I hadn’t got a car.
I didn’t have a car.
Had you got car?
She‘s got a nice apartment.
Have you got a computer?
I haven’t got any brothers or sisters.
I‘ve got a headache.
She‘s got a cold.
He‘s got brown hair and a long nose.
They‘ve got blue eyes.
We use was/were as the past simple forms of be. We use was for I/he/she/it and were for you/we/they.
I was at home yesterday.
You were late at the meeting.
She was excited about the party.
We were tired after the excursion.
Were they at the conference?
The negative short forms of was not and were not are wasn’t and weren’t, but there are no short forms of was and were in affirmative sentences.
Tom was tired, but Susan wasn’t very tired.
Paul and Tom were happy, but we weren’t happy.

title: Was/were: Use
description: Was/were are the past forms of am/is/are. Present: am/is = past: was. Present: are = past: were. I‘m happy. ⇒ I was happy. (am ⇒ was) She is at her office. ⇒ She was at her office. (is ⇒ was) You are late. ⇒ You were late. (are ⇒ were) We use was/were to talk about the past. We often use past expressions of time: yesterday, last night, last week, two months ago, etc. She was in Stuttgart last summer. We were late to the party last night. We use was/were in the expression was/were born. The expression be born is always past (NOT present): was/were born. ‘Where were you born?’ ‘I was born in Sweden.’ ‘Where are you born?’ ‘I am born in Sweden.’ Mozart was born in 1756.

title: Past simple regular
description: We often add -ed to verbs (regular verbs) to make the past simple. We often watch a film on Saturday. ⇒ We watched a film last Saturday. I live in Barcelona. ⇒ I lived in Barcelona in the 90s.

rules.title: When do we double the consonant?
rules.explanation: reFER ⇒ referred, preFER ⇒ preferred, reGRET ⇒ regretted

examples.sentence: We double the consonant when the verb ends in consonant + vowel + consonant. This is always true when the verb is one syllable.
stop ⇒ stopped, plan ⇒ planned, shop ⇒ shopped, rob ⇒ robbed.
When the verb is two syllables, we only double the consonant when the STRESS is in the last syllable.
But we do NOT double the consonant when the STRESS is NOT in the last syllable.
VIsit ⇒ visited, ANswer ⇒ answered.

title: Past simple irregular
description: Irregular verbs don’t add -ed to make the past simple. Some very common irregular verbs are:

title: Past simple: Use
description: We can use the past simple to talk about past finished actions or states. We know and we often mention when these actions happened with a past time expression: yesterday, yesterday morning, last night

rules.title: Past finished actions or states
rules.explanation: We can use the past simple to talk about past finished actions or states. We know and we often mention when these actions happened with a past time expression: yesterday, yesterday morning, last night, last week, two days ago, five years ago, etc.

examples.sentence: Jessica called me last night.
Rachel was a very good writer.

We can use the past simple to talk about habits or repeated actions that happened in the past but don’t happen in the present. We often use adverbs or expressions of frequency (often, always, every day, etc.).
When I was a child, I ate sweets every day.
In school, I always played football during break time.

title: Past simple negatives and questions
description: We use did and didn’t to make negatives, questions and short answers in past simple.

examples.sentence: Remember that the word order in questions is QWASI: (Question Word), Auxiliary (did), Subject, Infinitive.

rules.title: Infinitive without to
rules.formula: Did you go to the concert?

examples.sentence: We use did or didn’t with the bare infinitive of the main verb (= infinitive without to).
I didn’t work yesterday.
I didn’t didn’t worked yesterday.
Did you went to the concert?

rules.title: Don’t use did in positive sentences

examples.sentence: We use did or didn’t in negatives, questions and short answers, but NOT in positive sentences.
We stayed home yesterday.
We did stay home yesterday.
I went out last night.
I did go out last night.
We use did and didn’t (NOT was/wasn’t or were/weren’t) as the auxiliary verb in negatives and questions in past simple.
We didn’t eat at 6.
We weren’t eat at 6.
Were you do your homework?
We do NOT use did or didn’t in past simple negatives or questions when the main verb is be.
They weren’t happy.
They didn’t be happy.
Was he at home?
Did he be at home?

title: Will: Form
description: Will is an auxiliary verb. This means that: We use will + infinitive: I‘ll be (or will be) at home next Saturday. Will has a negative form: I won’t be (or will not be) at home next Saturday. We put will before the subject in questions: Will you be at home next Saturday? We use will in short answers: Yes, I will. / No, I won’t (or No, I will not).

title: Will: Use
description: We use will to talk about things that we think will happen in the future. He will love your new coat. They won’t win this match. 🔍 Note that it’s common to use will with the expressions I think, I don’t think, I hope, etc., or also with I’m sure, I’m not sure, perhaps, etc. I hope they will arrive soon. I don’t think they‘ll win. I’m sure you‘ll be fine. Perhaps I‘ll be late. We use will for promises or refusals. I will lend her the money. I won’t help you. We use will when we make decisions at the moment of speaking (instant decisions). A: There’s no milk in the fridge. B: I’ll go to the shop to buy some more. A: This box is very heavy. B: Don’t worry. I’ll help you.

title: Shall I…? Shall we…?
description: We use Shall I…? or I’ll when we offer to do things for other people. Shall I make some coffee? (=Do you want me to make coffee?) I‘ll make some coffee. We use Shall we…? to suggest doing something. Shall we go to the cinema? (=Do you want to go to the cinema?)

title: Be going to: Form
description: Be going to consists of the present continuous of the verb go (I’m going, you are going, etc.) + to-infinitive form of the main verb.

rules.title: When the main verb is go
rules.explanation: I’m going shopping this afternoon. (=I’m going to go shopping this afternoon.) We are going to Cyprus next summer. (=We are going to go to Cyprus next summer. )

examples.sentence: When the main verb is go, we can exclude it if we want.
We use be going to to talk about something that we see is going to happen (there is present evidence).
Don’t drive like a crazy man. We’re going to have an accident!
The doctor said I’m going to have a girl.
Look at those clouds. It’s going to rain.

rules.title: Intentions or plans
rules.explanation: ‘Why are you undressing?’ ‘Because I’m going to go for a swim.’

examples.sentence: We use be going to for intentions or plans (decisions taken before the moment of speaking).
We are going to have a drink after work. Do you want to come?
We often use be going to with future time expressions (tomorrow, next week, etc.).
We are going to play cards tonight.
She’s going to study biology next year.
We use can + infinitive without to.
I can swim.
I can to swim.
We can’t come.
We can’t to come.
We don’t add -s after he/she/it. We use can for all persons.
He can swim.
He cans swim.
Can is an auxiliary verb and has a negative and interrogative form. We don’t use do/don’t to make questions and negatives.
Can you swim?
Do you can swim?
He can’t swim.
He don’t can swim.
We use can/can’t to talk about ability in the present (=things that we know how to do).
Laura can sing very well.
I can’t speak German.
We use can/can’t to ask for permission or to say if something is possible or not.
Can I sit here?
You can’t vote if you aren’t 18 years old.
I’m sorry, but I can’t come to the party.
We use can/can’t to ask for something and to order in bars, restaurants, shops, etc.
Can you lend me some money?
Can I have some water, please?
Can I have a burger and a soda?

title: Form
description:
The form of the verb used for the imperative is the base form of the verb (=the infinitive without to). There is no subject.
The negative imperative is made with do not or don’t + infinitive (no subject).

title: Use
description: You can use the imperative to:

examples.sentence: Open the package carefully.
Connect the cable to the plug.

rules.title: Give orders
rules.explanation: Stop talking! Run!

examples.sentence: Fasten your seatbelt.

rules.title: Offer something or invite someone to something

examples.sentence: Have some cookies.
Come and sit with us.
Get some sleep.
Tell her how you feel.
Go and have fun.
Don’t touch that cable.
Please, Stop the car!

rules.title: Make suggestions
rules.formula:
You can use the form let’s + verb or let’s not + verb to make suggestions.

(+) It’s very hot today. Let’s go to the swimming pool.
(-) Let’s not go out today. I’m very tired.

title: Would you like…?
description: We use Would you like…? to offer something or to invite somebody to do something. Would you like…? = ‘Do you want…?’ Would you like some cake? (=Do you want some cake?) Would you like some coffee? Would you like to go to the cinema? (=Do you want to go to the cinema?) Where would you like to go?

title: I’d like
description: We use I’d like (=I would like) to talk about something that we want or something that we want to do. I’d like some water, please. (=I want some water.) I’d like more information about this product. I’d like to go to the zoo. (=I want to go to the zoo.) I’d like to have a lemonade.

title: Like
description: We use like + things or people to say that we think these things or people are nice or good. I like coffee. (=I think coffee is good.) I like John. (=I think John is nice.) We use like + -ing verb to say that we enjoy doing something. I like dancing. (=I enjoy dancing.) I like going to the cinema. (=I enjoy going to the cinema.) We use do in questions. Do you like coffee? Do you like going to the cinema?

title: Like vs Would like
description: Compare the following sentences: I like tea. (=I think tea is good.) I’d like some tea. (=I want some tea.) Do you like tea? (=Do you think tea is good?) Would you like some tea? (=Do you want some tea?) We use like + -ing verb or + to-infinitive and would like + to-infinitive. Compare: I like going to the cinema. (=I enjoy going to the cinema.) I’d like to go to the cinema. (=I want to go to the cinema.) Do you like going to the cinema? (=Do you enjoy going to the cinema?) Would you like to go to the cinema? (=Do you want to go to the cinema?)

title: Verbs + -ing
description: Some verbs in English are followed by another verb in the -ing form. The most common of these verbs are verbs of liking and disliking: love, like, enjoy, don’t mind, don’t like, hate. If we use another verb after these verbs, it often takes the -ing form. I love going to the gym. I like reading. I enjoy travelling. I don’t mind cooking. I don’t like doing homework. I hate getting up early. The second verb is in the -ing form in the past, too. When I was a child, I hated going to school. When I was a child, I hated went to school.

examples.sentence: You can check the spelling of the -ing form of the verbs in the table below.

title: Verbs + to-infinitive
description: Many verbs in English are followed by to-infinitive. Some of these verbs are forget, hope, learn, need, offer, plan, remember, start, want and would like. I forgot to turn off the light. I hope to see you soon. I‘m learning to drive. We need to arrive early. She offered to help us. We are planning to go to France. Remember to lock the door. I want to go home. I would like to buy a new computer. The second verb takes the to-infinitive form in the past too. I wanted to go home. I wanted to went home.

rules.title: Would like
rules.formula: The verb would like (or ‘d like) is different from like. We use would like + to + infinitive, and we normally use like + -ing verb to talk about general preference. You can learn more about this topic here.
rules.explanation: I‘d like to study English. (=I want to do it.) I like studying English. (=I enjoy it.)

examples.sentence: I‘d like to study English.
I like studying English.
The meaning is also different.
We use the question would you like… when we offer or suggest something.
Would you like a cup of coffee?
Would you like to dance with me?

title: A/an
description: We use a/an with singular nouns. This is a table. It’s an orange. We use a with nouns beginning with a consonant, and we use an with nouns beginning with a vowel (a, e, i, o, u). Give me an apple. There is an elephant in the zoo. It’s an amazing experience. We also use an with nouns beginning with h if the h is not pronounced. But we use a if the h is pronounced. Give me an hour. (The h is not pronounced in hour.) I want to buy a hat. (The h is pronounced in hat.) We use a and NOT an with nouns beginning with u when it is pronounced /ju:/, like university. Is there a university in this city? I wear a uniform.

examples.sentence: We do NOT use a/an with plural nouns.
These are tables.
These are a tables.
We add -s to make plural nouns in English. Sometimes we need to add -es, -ies or –ves. Check the spelling in the grammar chart above.
Some words have irregular plurals and do NOT add -s to make their plural form.

title: A/an, the, no article: Grammar chart

examples.sentence: We use a/an when we mention something for the first time.
I saw an old woman with a dog in the park.

rules.title: Jobs and descriptions
rules.explanation: ❗ Note that you cannot use singular countable nouns alone (without a, the, my, etc.)

examples.sentence: We use a/an to talk about people’s jobs or to say what kind of person or thing something/somebody is.
Paula is a teacher.
Paris is an interesting city.
When I was a teenager, I enjoyed sleeping.
I don’t have driving license.
I don’t have a driving license.
This hotel doesn’t have spa.
This hotel doesn’t have a spa.
We use the when we mention something for the second time.
A man and a woman sat in front of me. The man was British, but I think the woman wasn’t.

rules.title: Specific things
rules.explanation: ‘Where are the kids?’ ‘They’re in the garden.’ (=We know which kids and which garden.) Can you open the door? (=We know which door.)

examples.sentence: We use the to talk about specific things or people –when it’s clear which things or people we are talking about.
We use the when there’s only one of something.
This company is very profitable. The manager must be really good.
I’d like to live in this country, but not in the capital.
The moon looks beautiful today.

rules.title: Common places in town
rules.explanation: With places in a town where we commonly go (the park, the cinema, the doctor, etc.)

examples.sentence: I’m going to the bank.
I found Peter at the station.
I’m at the library.
We use the before superlative adjectives.
This is the best restaurant in town.
We use the before names of musical instruments.
Margaret plays the guitar and I play the flute.

title: Use no article
description: Compare: I love music. (=Music in general.) I loved the music at the party. (=Specific music.) Children are often difficult. (=Children in general.) They are a nice family. The children are very funny

rules.title: General meaning
rules.explanation: Compare: I love music. (=Music in general.) I loved the music at the party. (=Specific music.) Children are often difficult. (=Children in general.) They are a nice family. The children are very funny. (=The children in that family.)

examples.sentence: We do not use an article before plural or uncountable nouns to talk about something in general.
I don’t drink milk.
Women drive more cautiously than men.
We do not use an article before the names of meals: breakfast, lunch, or dinner.
Dinner is served at 8.
I always have breakfast with my children.
We do not use an article before names of years, months and days of the week.
Friday is my favourite day of the week.
I think 2020 will be an excellent year.

rules.title: TV
rules.explanation: We do not use an article with TV (when used as a broadcasting service, NOT as an appliance) ❗ But: Turn off the TV. I’ve bought a new TV.

examples.sentence: I saw it on TV.
I don’t watch TV.

rules.title: Names of languages and school subjects

examples.sentence: We do not use an article before the names of languages and school subjects.
He doesn’t speak English.
I study biology.

rules.title: Next, last
rules.explanation: ❗ Compare: We really loved London. The last day we were really sad. (The last day= the last day of that trip. NOT the day before now)

examples.sentence: We do not use an article before next and last + time expression (when they mean before or after now).
The meeting is next Thursday.
I saw him last week.
Last year we spent one week in London (Last year= the year before now).

title: This/these= here, that/those= there
description: We use this and these for things that are here (near). This is singular and these is plural. This is my new watch. These are my sisters Anna and Jenny. We use that and those for things that are there (not near). That is singular and those is plural. That man is very strange. I need those boxes over there. We can use this, that, these, those with a noun (this box, that car, etc.) or without a noun. This watch is very expensive. I like that woman in the park. Who is that? (= that person) ‘What are those?’ ‘They are old pictures.’ We use this is when we introduce people and on the telephone. Hi, this is Tim. (Tim on the telephone) Laura: ‘Hi, Sara. This is my friend Paul.’ Sara: ‘Hi, Paul. Nice to meet you.’

examples.sentence: We use subject pronoun + verb, and we use possessive adjective + noun.
These are Susan and Thomas; they are from Ohio. And that’s their house.
I love my friend Sheila.
Adjectives in English have no plural form. Possessive adjectives are always singular.
These are their suitcases.
These are theirs suitcases.

rules.title: It/its for things, they/their for people and things

examples.sentence: We use he/his and she/her for people, and we use it/its for things. But in the plural, we use they/their for people and things.
The hotel has its own spa. It is a relaxing place.
I like those chairs, they are beautiful. But their legs are too long.

rules.title: It’s for time and weather
rules.explanation: ‘What time is it?’ ‘It’s three o’clock.’

examples.sentence: We use it’s (=it is) to talk about the time or the weather.
It’s very windy today, but it’s sunny.

rules.title: Its or it’s?
rules.formula: Its = possessive adjective. It’s = it is (subject + verb be)

examples.sentence: Look at that dog. It’s beautiful. I like its hair.

title: Object pronouns vs subject pronouns: Me or I, She or Her?

examples.sentence: Subject pronouns are the subject of a verb. They go before the verb.
I like your dress.
You are late.
He is my friend.
It often rains.
She is on holiday.
We live in England.
They come from London.
Object pronouns are the object of a verb. They go after the verb.
Can you help me , please?
I can see you.
She doesn’t like him.
I see her every Monday.
We can see him, but he can’t see us.
We use object pronouns after prepositions.
She is there for me.
I’ll get it for you.
Give it to him.
Can you listen to her?
Don’t take it from us.
I want to speak to them.

title: Countable nouns
description: Countable nouns are nouns that we can count: car, house, book, etc. We can say one car, two cars, three cars, etc. Countable nouns have singular and plural forms: a car/cars, a house/houses, a book/books, etc.

examples.sentence: We CANNOT use a singular countable noun without a determiner like a/an, the, my, your, her, etc.
I have car.
I have a car.
When I was child.
When I was a child.

title: Uncountable nouns
description: Uncountable nouns are nouns that we cannot count: money, milk, rain, etc. We cannot say one money, two moneys, etc. So, uncountable nouns do not have a plural form; they only have a singular form: money, milk, rain, etc.

examples.sentence: We cannot use a/an + uncountable noun. A/an means ‘one’, and we cannot count uncountable nouns.
I need a money.
I need money.
We need to buy a sugar.
We need to buy sugar.

rules.title: Types of words that are uncountable
rules.explanation:
Some types of words that are typically uncountable are: Food, drinks and liquids: cheese, bread, pasta, coffee, milk, petrol, fuel, etc.

Materials: iron, wood, metal, paper, plastic, etc.

Abstract ideas and feelings: information, advice, strength, time, love, excitement, etc.

Illnesses: diabetes, Alzheimer’s, cancer, etc.

Languages: English, French, Spanish, etc.

rules.title: Uncountable in English but not in other languages

examples.sentence: Some nouns are uncountable in English, but they are countable in other languages. Some of them are advice, news (it ends in -s, but it’s a singular word), furniture, luggage, baggage, bread, cheese, toast, etc.

title: Nouns that can be countable and uncountable
description: Some nouns can be countable and uncountable because they can refer to a unit or to ‘mass’ or ‘material’. Compare: Yesterday I had two coffees. (= two cups of coffee) I love coffee. (= the liquid that we drink) I found one hair in my soup. (one single hair) She has beautiful hair. (= the mass of hair on her head)

examples.sentence: We use a/an + singular countable noun.
I have a new car.
She has a brother and a sister.
We cannot use a/an before a plural noun or an uncountable noun.
We need to buy a sugar.
I need to buy sugar.
We saw a very beautiful places.
We saw very beautiful places.
We use some and any before countable plural nouns or uncountable singular nouns.
He gave me some coins.
He didn’t give me any coins.
He gave me some money.
We use some in positive sentences.
We cooked some cookies.

rules.title: Any
rules.explanation: ❗ But we use some in questions when we are asking for something, or we are offering something. Can I have some tea? (=I’m asking for some tea.) Would you like some tea? (=I’m offering you some tea.)

examples.sentence: We use any in negative sentences and questions.
She didn’t send me any messages.
Have you got any brothers or sisters?

title: Grammar chart: Much, many, a lot of, a little, a few, no, any, none

rules.title: Many for countable, much for uncountable in (?) (-)

examples.sentence: We use much/many in negative sentences and questions. We use many before plural countable nouns and much before uncountable nouns. We don’t normally use them in affirmative sentences.
There isn’t much coffee in the jar.
Were there many people at the party?

rules.title: How much/how many
rules.formula: How many books did you read last semester?
rules.explanation: ‘How much is it?’ ‘It’s 43 pounds.’ ‘How much are the trousers?’ ‘They’re 58 pounds.’

examples.sentence: We use how many + plural nouns and how much + uncountable nouns to ask about quantity. You can review countable and uncountable nouns here.
How much coffee do you drink every day?
We can also say How much is it? to ask about the price of an item.
We use a lot of before both plural countable and uncountable nouns to talk about a large quantity. We normally use a lot of in positive sentences.
She spends a lot of time watching TV.
We had lots of good moments together.
We can say quite a lot of to talk about medium quantity.
With my new job, I have quite a lot of free time.
It is also possible to use a lot of in negative sentences and questions.
Do you eat a lot of sugar?
I don’t read a lot of books.

rules.title: Of before noun; of at the end of sentence
rules.formula: ‘How many beers did you have?’ ‘I don’t know; I had a lot.’
rules.explanation: ‘How much coffee did you have?’ ‘A lot.’

examples.sentence: We must always use a lot of including of before a noun. However, we can use a lot (without of) at the end of a sentence or in short answers.
I like her a lot.

rules.title: A few for countable; a little for uncountable

examples.sentence: We use a few before plural countable nouns and a little before uncountable nouns in affirmative, negative and interrogative sentences to talk about a small quantity.
I have to do a few things this afternoon.
I always put a little milk in my tea.
We can also use not many + plural countable or not much + uncountable nouns. The meaning is similar.
I don’t have to do many things this afternoon.
I don’t put much milk in my tea.

rules.title: No/not…any/none
rules.explanation: ‘How much time do you have?’ ‘None.’

examples.sentence: When we want to talk about zero quantity, we can use no + noun or not…any + noun. The meaning is the same.
I have no time today.
I don’t have any time today.
In short answers, we use none.

title: Whose, possessive ‘s: Whose is this? It’s Mike’s
description: We use ‘s to show that something belongs to a person (or a pet) or to talk about relationships between people. This is Peter‘s father. This is the father of Peter. Peter and Mary‘s car is red. My cat‘s ears are white. We also use the possessive ‘s to talk about shops and houses. I am at John‘s. (= at John’s house) I need to go to the chemist‘s. (= the chemist’s shop) Singular noun + ‘s Anna’s clothes, Chris’s wife, the student’s books, etc. Plural noun ending in -s + ‘ (apostrophe) The students’ books, the boys’ toys, the teachers’ lounge, etc. Irregular plural noun (NOT ending in -s) + ‘s The children’s toys, men’s clothes, etc. When one thing belongs to two or more people, add ‘s only after the last noun. Paul and Katherine’s house, Anna and George’s car, etc. When we have two or more people, and each person has one thing, we add ‘s after each person. Anna’s and George’s cars (Anna’s car and George’s car), Sally’s and Tim’s computers (Sally’s computer and Tim’s computer). We use of and NOT ‘s when we talk about things (and not people or pets). the end of the street the street‘s end a picture of the eclipse the eclipse‘s picture the man of the match the match‘s man With nouns that are used together very often, we often use them together without of and without ‘s a school bus the city centre the car key

rules.title: When do we use whose?
rules.formula: When we answer with the possessive ‘s, we can also use ‘s + noun or ‘s without a noun.
rules.explanation: We use the question word whose to ask about possession. There are two possible forms: whose + noun whose without a noun It’s John‘s car It’s John‘s.

examples.sentence: Whose car is this?
Whose books are those?
Whose is this car?
Whose are those books?
Whose is this car?

rules.title: Whose vs who’s
rules.explanation: Whose = possession (of who) Who’s = who is

examples.sentence: Whose car is this?
Who’s car is this?
Who’s that man in the lobby?
Whose that man in the lobby?

title: There is, there are: Use
description: We use there is, there are to say that something exists. There is a lot of noise.

examples.sentence: We often use there is, there are to say where something is.
There is a good restaurant at the end of the street.
There are some keys in that box.
We can also use  there is, there are to say when something happens.
There is a concert tonight.
There are a lot of storms in summer.

rules.title: A, some, any
rules.formula: We often use  there is, there are + a, some, any.

rules.title: There is + singular / there are + plural
rules.formula: We use there is + singular noun, and there are + plural noun.

examples.sentence: There is a new teacher in the school.
There are some problems that we need to solve.
We use there is + a list of things if the first noun after there is is singular.
In the room, there is a bed, a wardrobe and two chairs.

title: There is, there are, there was, there were: Form

examples.sentence: The past form of there is, there are is there was, there were.
There is a car in the street. ⇒  There was a car in the street.
There are some cars in the street. ⇒  There were some cars in the street.
The short form of there is is there’s, but we don’t use a short form of there are. There’s is not used in short answers.
There’s a new secretary in the office.
There are two bathrooms in this house.

There are no short forms for there was/there were.

In negative sentences, we often use the short forms there isn’t/there aren’t and there wasn’t/weren’t.
There isn’t any bread left. We need to buy more.
There aren’t any cookies in the jar.

title: There
description: We use there to say when or where something is. There are some dirty cups on the table. There is a party tomorrow. We use there to say that something exists or is happening. There is a problem. There was an accident. Note that we use there the first time we mention something. If we talk about the same thing again, we use it or they. There are some cups on the table. But they are dirty. There was an accident. It was horrible.

title: It
description: We use it to talk about something again in the conversation. There is a party tomorrow. It will be fantastic! We saw a snake in the forest. It was very big. We use it to talk about times and dates. It‘s my mum’s birthday today. Let’s go home. It‘s very late. It‘s four o’clock. We use it to talk about the weather. It‘s very windy. It‘s a nice day. It rained a lot yesterday. It‘s four degrees. We use it to talk about distance. How far is it? It‘s not very far. It‘s a long way to the nearest petrol station. It‘s 2 kilometres from the hotel to the station. ❗ Note that we use far in negative sentences and questions, and we use a long way in affirmative sentences. We use it’s + adjective + to-infinitive. It’s nice to see you again. It was difficult to find the hotel.

title: Be careful with these common mistakes!
description: I think the worst part of the exam it’s speaking. I think the worst part of the exam is speaking. We do NOT use it when the subject of the verb is in the sentence. In the sentence above, the subject of is is ‘the worst part of the exam’. I love their music. Is fantastic! I love their music. It’s fantastic! Don’t forget to use it when you mention something again in the conversation.

title: The difference between this and it
description: You can use this to talk about something near you. It is like pointing your finger at something in front of you. This cat is cute. (The cat is right there with you.) This is my house. (The house in front of you.) I think this jacket is perfect for you. You can also use this to introduce a person to another one or to introduce yourself on a phone call. This is my brother John. Hello, this is Linda. (On the phone) The plural form of this is these. These are my brothers, John and Edward.

rules.title: To talk about something again
rules.explanation: We use it to talk about something that we mentioned, or that is known. It is a pronoun, like he or she, but we use he or she to talk about people, and we use it to talk about objects or animals. There was a storm, and they cancelled the concert. It was a disaster. (It refers to the storm and the cancelling of the concert)

examples.sentence: This cat is cute. It is sleeping.
This is my house. I bought it last year.
Apart from objects or animals, we can also use it to refer to a situation or idea mentioned before.

rules.title: Times, dates, weather, distance
rules.explanation: “What day is it?” “It‘s Tuesday.”

examples.sentence: It is used to talk about times, dates, weather, and distances.
It‘s very late.
It was sunny yesterday.
It‘s not very far from here.
It‘s nice to see you again.
It‘s easy to get lost in this part of the city.
It‘s important that you learn this.
When we use adjectives with a noun in English, the adjectives go before the noun.
It’s an expensive guitar.
It’s a guitar expensive.
She has a new car.
She has a car new.
We can also use adjectives without a noun after the verb be.
This guitar is expensive.
Her car is new.
In questions, the adjective can go after be + subject.
Is your car new?
Are your classmates nice?
We can also use adjectives without a noun after the verbs of the senses: feel, look, smell, sound, taste.
Your idea sounds interesting.
I don’t like this soup. It tastes horrible.
You look tired.
These cookies smell delicious.
I feel depressed.
Adjectives in English have no plural. They are always singular.
These are my favourite shoes.
These are my favourites shoes.
She has blue eyes.
She has blues eyes.
We can use very and quite before the adjectives.
It’s very expensive.
It’s quite expensive.

title: Adverbs of manner
description: It rained heavily. (=We are describing how it rained.) He always replies quickly. (=We are describing how he replies)

rules.title: Adverbs of manner: Use
rules.explanation: It rained heavily. (=We are describing how it rained.) He always replies quickly. (=We are describing how he replies)

examples.sentence: We use adverbs of manner after a verb to describe the verb. We use an adverb of manner to say how something happens or how we do something.

rules.title: Adverbs of manner: Position
rules.formula: We use adverbs of manner after the verb or, if there is an object, after verb + object.

examples.sentence: He drives carefully.
She plays the guitar well.
We can use very, really, quite before an adverb of manner.
It rained quite heavily.
He drives quite carefully.
She plays the guitar really well.

title: Adjectives
description: We use adjectives before a noun or after the verb be. You can review the topic of adjectives here. This is an expensive guitar. This guitar is expensive.

title: Adverbs of manner vs adjectives
description: We use adjectives to describe a noun (before a noun or after the verb be), and we use adverbs of manner to describe a verb (after the verb or verb + object). Compare: Robert plays the guitar well. (=W

rules.title: Difference
rules.explanation: We use adjectives to describe a noun (before a noun or after the verb be), and we use adverbs of manner to describe a verb (after the verb or verb + object). Compare: Robert plays the guitar well. (=We are describing the verb, i.e. how Robert plays the guitar.) Robert is a good guitar player. (=We are describing the noun, Robert.)

examples.sentence: Sara eats slowly.
Sara is a slow eater.
Some words end in -ly, but they are adjectives, NOT adverbs: friendly, lovely, silly. We CANNOT transform these adjectives into adverbs of manner.

title: Comparative adjectives
description: We use more + adjective + than or adjective + -er + than to compare things or people. My car is more expensive than your car. I am older than my brother. In this chart, you can see when we need to use more … than or -er than and the changes in spelling.

examples.sentence: We use the comparative form of an adjective to compare two things.
Luke is taller than Mathew.
This armchair is more comfortable than the sofa.

rules.title: Less … than
rules.formula: When we compare two things, we can also use the form less + adjective + than (less ≠ more).
rules.explanation: Peter is less popular than Marta. (= Marta is more popular than Peter.)

examples.sentence: If we use a personal pronoun after than, we need an object pronoun (me, you, him, etc.).
My sister is taller than me.
His sister is more intelligent than him.
Before the comparative (more or –er), we can use much (=big difference) or a bit (=small difference).
He’s a bit taller than me.
Switzerland is much more expensive than Italy.
We use more or –er, but we cannot use more + -er.
My brother is more taller than me.
My brother is taller than me.
After a comparative adjective, we use than and NOT that.
My brother is taller that me.
My brother is taller than me.

rules.title: Than + second element of the comparison

examples.sentence: We use than + the second thing that we are comparing. When we don’t mention the second element of the comparison, we do NOT use than.
I am tall, but my brother is taller than.
I am tall, but my brother is taller.
We use the superlative form of an adjective or adverb to compare three or more things.
Both John and his brother play football, but John is better.
John and his two brothers all play football, but John is the best.

rules.title: The best in
rules.explanation: After the superlative we use in before names of places or before singular words referring to groups of people (class, school, team, family, etc.)

examples.sentence: The Everest is the highest mountain of the world.
The Everest is the highest mountain in the world.
She is the best student in the class.
He’s the tallest in the family.

rules.title: The / my / John’s
rules.explanation: Before the superlative we always use the or a possessive adjective (my, your, his, etc.) or noun + possessive ‘s (Paul’s, Elisabeth’s, etc.)

examples.sentence: He is the best.
This is my most expensive jacket.
This is Paul’s best friend.

title: And, but, or, so, because: Grammar chart
And
description: We use and for adding similar information or ideas; it means ‘too’ or ‘in addition’. My brother is a plumber, and my sister is a teacher. They have an apartment in the city and a little house in the countryside. When we are connecting sentences, we don’t need to repeat the subject (Tom, I, we, she, etc.) She got up and left. (=She got up and she left.) He was looking at us and smiling. (=He was looking at us and he was smiling.)

title: But
description: We use but for adding contrasting information, i.e. information that is different. I studied a lot, but I failed the exam. He is good but not the best.

title: Or
description: We use or to connect different possibilities. Would you like to go to the swimming pool or to the beach? He can go to a hotel or stay with us. We use or (NOT and) in negative sentences to mean ‘not one thing and not another thing’. I don’t like oranges and lemons. I don’t like oranges or lemons.

rules.title: Use and, but, or to connect similar words or phrases.
rules.explanation: The items that we connect with and, but, or should be the same grammar type. Check these common mistakes: I like dancing and singing.  (-ing verb and -ing verb) Would you like to go to the beach or to have an ice cream in the park?  (to-infinitive and to-infinitive)

examples.sentence: I like dancing and sing.
Would you like to go to the beach or having an ice cream in the park?

title: So
description: We use so to introduce the consequence or the result of something. The second part of the sentence is the result of the first part of the sentence. It was cold, so I turned on the heating. I spent three hours at the gym, so I’m really tired now.

title: Because
description: We use because to introduce the reason for something. I turned on the heating because it was cold. I’m speaking quietly because I don’t want to wake up the baby.

title: At, in, on: Prepositions of time
description: Use at with times of day, including mealtimes, bedtime, etc. at 3 o’clock, at 10.30 am, at noon, at dinnertime, at bedtime, at sunrise, at sunset, at the moment Use at in the following common expressions: at the weekend: I don’t usually work at the weekend. at Christmas/Easter: I stay with my family at Christmas. at the same time: We finished the test at the same time. at present/at the moment: He’s not home at present. Try later. Use in with months, seasons, years, decades, centuries and long periods of time in general. in May, in the summer, in 1990, in the 1990s, in the 20th century, in the Ice Age, in the past/future Use on followed by days and dates. on Sunday, on Tuesday mornings, on 6 March, on 25 December 2010, on Christmas Day, on Independence Day, on my birthday, on New Year’s Eve in the morning in the afternoon in the evening at night When we say last, next, every, this, we do not use at, in, on. I went to London last June. I went to London in last June. He’s coming back next Tuesday. He’s coming back on next Tuesday. I go home every Easter. I go home at every Easter. We’ll call you this evening. We’ll call you in this evening.

title: At, in, on: Prepositions of place
At
description: Can you see that car at the traffic light? (=The car is at a point near the traffic light) Who is that man at the door? (=The man is at a point near the door)

rules.title: At a point
rules.explanation: Can you see that car at the traffic light? (=The car is at a point near the traffic light) Who is that man at the door? (=The man is at a point near the door)

examples.sentence: We use at to refer to a point near something.

rules.title: At the top of/at the bottom of/at the end of

examples.sentence: We use at in the expressions at the top of, at the bottom of and at the end of.
She is at the top of the stairs.
Please sign at the bottom of the page.
The new café is at the end of the street.
We use at to refer to group activities like parties, concerts, and other events.
We were at the cinema.
I didn’t see you at Jackie’s party.
We met at a concert.
We normally use at with school, university and college.
He is at school every morning until 12.
I’m studying at Oxford University.

rules.title: Be at home/work
rules.explanation: We say be at home/at work (without the)

examples.sentence: She’s at home.
She’s at the home.
I’ll be at work all morning.
I’ll be at the work all morning.
We use at to refer to shops, restaurants, cafés, etc.
I’m at the bakery.
If you are at the chemist’s, can you buy some aspirin?
We use in to refer to a position inside of a three-dimensional space.
The book is in the bag.
She is waiting in the classroom.
We also use in for areas that have limits or boundaries, like continents, countries, cities, regions, etc.
We are in France.
I love the houses in the Alps.
We use in for cars and vans.
They are in the car.
We also use in when something is in the water: in the sea, in the river, in the swimming pool, etc.
The kids have fun in the swimming pool.

rules.title: In a picture, in a book

examples.sentence: We also use in for things that are printed in books, pictures, documents, etc.
Who’s that woman in the picture?
Does it say anything about the concert in the newspaper?
We use on to refer to a position on a surface.
The book is on the table.
We use on to refer to floors in a building.
The office is on the third floor.
We use on in the expressions on the right and on the left.
The office is on the third floor on the left.
We use on when we are using public transport: on the bus, on a train, on a plane, etc.
She’s on the bus right now.
We use on when we read, see or learn something in the media.
I saw it on TV last night.
I learned it on the internet.

title: Next to, under, between, in front of, behind, over, etc.

examples.sentence: We had a picnic next to/beside the river.
Come and sit next to/beside me.
I live in a small town between Liverpool and Manchester.
She is sleeping between her mum and dad.
John sits in front of me in class.
You are always in front of the mirror.
She is hiding behind the curtains.
We hang our coats behind the door.

rules.title: Opposite
rules.explanation: I live opposite the supermarket (=on the other side of the road) I am sitting opposite him (=facing him)

examples.sentence: The cat is hiding under the bed.
Why are your shoes under the table?
I’m wearing a white shirt under the jersey.
Above and over mean ‘at or to a higher position’. Over is more common.
She lives in an apartment above/over the grocery store.
There is a shelf above/over the sink.

title: Word order in present simple questions
description: The order of words in an English question in present simple is QWASI: (Question Word) + Auxiliary + Subject + Infinitive. What do you do? Where do they live? In Yes-No questions (questions that we answer saying yes or no) there is NO Question Word. Do you speak English? Does she live here? The auxiliary verb in present simple is be or do/does. Below, you can see a grammar chart on the order of words in present simple questions with be or do/does.

rules.title: Questions with am/is/are
rules.formula: When the verb in the sentence is be, we use am/is/are as the auxiliary verb before the subject. Then the word order is QWAS: (Question Word) + Auxiliary (am/is/are) + Subject

rules.title: Questions with do/does
rules.formula:
When we have a verb that is not be, we use do/does as the auxiliary verb.
We use does with he/she/it and do with I/you/we/they. Remember that the main verb after the subject is in the infinitive form (NOT -s or -es).

examples.sentence: What does she do?
What does she does?
Where does he work?
Where does he works?
We often use the question word what + noun as a question word: what time, what colour, what size, etc.
What colour is your jacket?
What size do you want?
What car does he have?
We can also use how + adjective/adverb as a question word in phrases like how often, how old, how tall, etc.
How fast is your car?
How big is her house?
How often do you play sports?

title: Adverbs of frequency
description: We use adverbs of frequency with the present simple to talk about how often something happens.

rules.title: Position
rules.explanation: In negative sentences, the adverb of frequency goes after don’t/doesn’t:

examples.sentence: Adverbs of frequency go before the verb but after the verb be.
We don’t often go to bed late.
He doesn’t always agree with me.
In questions, adverbs of frequency go after the subject.
Is he always late?
Do they often go to the library?

rules.title: Never/hardly ever
rules.explanation: The adverbs never and hardly ever (=almost never) have a negative meaning, but they are used with a positive verb:

examples.sentence: He is never late.
He isn’t never late.
They hardly ever go to the library.
They don’t hardly ever go to the library.

title: Expressions of frequency
description: There are other expressions that we use to talk about frequency: once a day, twice a week, three times a month, every day, etc. These expressions are longer (2 words or more), and they go at the end of the sentence. I brush my teeth three times a day. I see her every day.

title: Basic word order in English: Grammar chart
description: In English, we normally need to include the subject in a sentence. The subject goes before the verb. We dance. She is waiting. We use it or they as a subject to talk about something that we mentioned before. I like your guitar. Is nice. I like your guitar. It is nice. Can you see those trees? Are beautiful. Can you see those trees? They are beautiful. We don’t include the subject in imperative sentences. Sit down, please. Call me soon. We can put an adverb of frequency between the subject and the verb. BUT adverbs of frequency go after the verb when the verb is be. We often dance. BUT: You are always late. When there is an object in the sentence, we need to put it after the verb. Verb and object are together. I like very much pizza. I like pizza very much. She plays every day the guitar. She plays the guitar every day. We normally say ‘where’ and ‘when’ something happens in this order. She arrives in the morning at the hospital. She arrives at the hospital in the morning. Will you be at 3 pm in the office? Will you be in the office at 3 pm?
